title: Write to understand and to transfer
html:
Writing in this form is a merciless way to spot the holes, the quiet disconnections in your understanding. You can nod along to an idea in your head and feel that you grasp it, but the moment you try to set it down in full sentences, every gap announces itself. Serious note takers call these permanent notes — notes meant to last, written to stand on their own.

The process of writing in this way should feel effortful, but at the same time playful. It is effortful because you are deliberately filling the gaps and establishing meaningful connections to your existing ideas and mental models, which is real cognitive work and resists being rushed. It is playful because, in the very act of doing that, you stumble onto new insights and new ideas you did not know you had — the connections you forge end up teaching you something.

The objective of this writing is that you, in the future, can read these notes back and still comprehend them without feeling that they are banal or empty. If you fail to meet that bar — or meet it only poorly — it is a sign your threshold for concretizing the idea was set too low to ever really comprehend the subject. A note that reads as obvious later usually means you never pinned the thought down in the first place. Hence why writing to understand should equate to writing to transfer, whether the recipient is your future self or someone else entirely.

Writing to understand has a mutual relationship with Read with intention. “To write” gives a purpose to “to read”: knowing you intend to write something down changes how attentively, and how hungrily, you read in the first place. (A practice I keep returning to in a note from a friend.)

backlinks.title: Different purposes of writing
backlinks.context: I splits the act of writing into two different purposes, Write to attend and Write to understand and to transfer.
